summary: DHH argues that while tech luminaries like Musk and Hawking worry about future AI threats like Skynet, AI-powered algorithms are already causing real harm today. Social media platforms optimize for addiction and anxiety, airlines use algorithms to split families for profit, and ride-sharing companies exploit cognitive biases. He focuses his sharpest criticism on Predictim, a startup that claims to use AI to vet babysitters by analyzing their social media accounts, calling it dangerous snake oil that doesn't work but still invades privacy. He concludes that we should focus on fighting today's algorithmic abuses rather than hypothetical future ones.
key_points: Worrying about hypothetical future AI threats distracts from the real algorithmic harms happening right now
Social media algorithms are optimized to exploit psychological vulnerabilities for engagement and ad revenue
Predictim exemplifies the worst of AI snake oil: promising safety through social media surveillance while disclaiming all responsibility
Consumers are disturbingly eager to accept invasive AI-branded tools, partly because hype about superintelligent AI makes them overestimate current capabilities
We have the power to reject, regulate, and ridicule harmful AI applications today, unlike a hypothetical future Skynet scenario
money_quotes: We don't need to wait for the singularity before artificial intelligence becomes capable of turning the world into a dystopian nightmare.
We're made increasingly miserable because connect-the-world imperialists are unleashing machine learning on our most vulnerable and base impulses.
There's no way this even fucking works! Simply no way.
This is that infamous Silicon Valley style of promising the world yet accepting zero responsibility for the consequences of its actions.

When you have Musk and Hawking waving their hands wildly about how The Great AI is going to render us all slaves so soon that we need emergency legislation about it, I can't fault people for thinking that it's already good enough to assess the personality of a babysitter.
Chances are good that when we do, we see it's just two rubber bands of questionable ethics holding together four cans of bullshit.
We can chose to reject, regulate, and ridicule when AI-branded algorithms turn against our better nature.
key_insight: The real AI threat isn't a future superintelligence but today's algorithms that exploit human psychology for profit while hiding behind disclaimers and hype about capabilities they don't have.
